title: The Safety and Quality of Health Care

items.subtopic: The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Errors: Slips vs. Mistakes in Healthcare
items.content: In healthcare, errors can be categorized into slips and mistakes. Slips are low-level, semiautomatic errors that occur during routine tasks, such as forgetting to complete an order due to interruption. Mistakes involve higher-level decision-making errors, often occurring in unfamiliar situations, like incorrect dosing of a new medication.
keyPoints: Slips: Errors from routine, automatic actions (e.g., forgetting an action due to interruption).
Mistakes: Errors from conscious decisions in non-routine situations (e.g., dosing unfamiliar medications).
Prevention strategies differ for slips and mistakes.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.title: Impact of Fatigue on Healthcare Errors
items.content: Fatigue is a significant factor increasing the likelihood of errors in healthcare. Studies show that house officers are about one-third more likely to make errors after a 24-hour shift compared to when they have had sufficient rest. The implementation of an 80-hour workweek aims to mitigate fatigue-related errors, though extended-duty shifts remain a critical issue.
keyPoints: Fatigue increases error likelihood; house officers are ~33% more likely to err post-24-h shift.
American College of Graduate Medical Education implemented the 80-h workweek to reduce fatigue.
Extended-duty shifts continue to be a major cause of fatigue-related errors.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.title: Role of Interruptions in Healthcare Errors
items.content: Interruptions are frequent in healthcare settings and significantly increase the likelihood of errors. Common scenarios include forgetting to complete tasks when interrupted by a page or call. Minimizing interruptions and using tools to assess the urgency of interruptions can help reduce these errors.
keyPoints: Interruptions frequently lead to incomplete actions, increasing error rates.
Minimizing interruptions and prioritizing their urgency can mitigate error risk.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.title: Adverse Event Frequency and Preventability
items.content: The frequency of adverse events in healthcare settings, particularly inpatient care, is significant. The Harvard Medical Practice Study found a 3.7% rate of adverse events, with 58% deemed preventable. Subsequent studies suggest the frequency may now be as high as one in four admissions due to improved detection methods and increased complexity of care.
keyPoints: Adverse event rate: 3.7%
Preventability: 58% of adverse events
Recent estimates suggest harm approaches one in four admissions
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.title: Common Adverse Events and Their Impact
items.content: Adverse drug events (ADEs) are the most common adverse event, accounting for 19% of all cases. Other frequent issues include wound infections (14%) and technical complications (13%). Almost half of adverse events occur in surgical settings, with ADEs being a leading error category post-discharge.
keyPoints: ADEs: 19% of adverse events
Wound infections: 14%
Technical complications: 13%
Surgical procedure association: ~50% of adverse events
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.title: Prevention Strategies for Adverse Events
items.content: Checklists have significantly reduced nosocomial infections in intensive care units, particularly catheter-associated bloodstream infections and ventilator-associated pneumonia. While strategies to reduce ADEs exist, demonstrating a clinically meaningful reduction remains challenging.
keyPoints: Checklist use reduces catheter-associated bloodstream infection
Reduction also seen in ventilator-associated pneumonia
ADE prevention strategies show mixed results on overall rate reduction
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.title: Impact of CPOE with Clinical Decision Support on Medication Errors
items.content: Computerized physician order entry (CPOE) systems, especially when linked with clinical decision support, significantly reduce serious medication errors. These systems help by suggesting default doses, ensuring complete orders, and checking for potential issues like allergies or drug interactions.
keyPoints: CPOE reduces serious medication error rate by 55%.
Decision support improves dose appropriateness in renal insufficiency from one-third to two-thirds.
Patients with renal insufficiency discharged half a day earlier with decision support.
Over 95% of U.S. hospitals had implemented CPOE as of 2019.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.title: Role of Bar Coding and Smart Pumps in Medication Safety
items.content: Bar coding linked with electronic medication administration records ensures correct patient, medication, and timing. Smart pumps provide alerts for potentially dangerous dosing errors, enhancing safety.
keyPoints: Bar coding helps ensure the right patient receives the right medication at the right time.
Smart pumps alert health care professionals to high doses.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.title: Challenges in Measuring Safety and Reporting Adverse Events
items.content: Measuring safety is challenging due to the rarity of adverse events. Spontaneous reporting systems are underutilized, capturing only ~1 in 20 ADEs. Electronic records may offer better detection methods.
keyPoints: Adverse events are rare, making measurement difficult and expensive.
Spontaneous reporting captures only ~1 in 20 adverse drug events (ADEs).
Electronic record searches show promise for identifying adverse events.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.title: Understanding Quality of Care: Donabedian's Framework
items.content: Quality of care can be categorized into structure, process, and outcome. Structure refers to the characteristics applicable in a setting, such as having specific facilities or using electronic health records. Process involves how care is delivered, like performing procedures at recommended intervals. Outcome measures what happens after care, such as mortality rates.
keyPoints: Quality of care categorized into structure, process, and outcome.
Good structure and process do not guarantee good outcomes.
Improving overall quality may be more effective by raising all providers' performance levels rather than targeting poor performers.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.title: Continuous Quality Improvement: The Plan-Do-Check-Act Cycle
items.content: Organizations should continuously evaluate and improve their care delivery processes using the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle. This method allows for rapid improvements, such as reducing time between diagnosis and treatment, by making small iterative changes.
keyPoints: Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle is crucial for continuous quality improvement.
Statistical tools like control charts help assess progress in process improvements.
Continuous evaluation and small changes can lead to significant long-term improvements.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.title: Factors Affecting Quality of Care
items.content: Quality is influenced by provider stress, production pressure levels, and system quality. High or low production pressures can lead to omissions in care or boredom, respectively, while poor systems hinder even the most dedicated providers.
keyPoints: Provider stress and production pressure levels affect care quality.
Poor systems significantly impact performance regardless of provider dedication.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.title: Improving Quality with System Changes
items.content: Quality in healthcare can be enhanced by modifying systems of care, such as implementing reminders and bundles for specific actions. These strategies have shown effectiveness in improving performance, particularly in preventive services and reducing ventilator-associated pneumonia.
keyPoints: Reminders improve delivery of preventive services.
Bundles include groups of quality measures (e.g., head of bed elevation) that enhance ICU care.
SCAMPs are standardized guidelines to streamline clinical workflows.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.title: Chronic Disease Management and Cost-Effectiveness
items.content: The Chronic Care Model emphasizes a team-based approach for chronic disease management, focusing on strategies like self-management support and decision support. Evidence supports the effectiveness of team changes and case managers in reducing hemoglobin A1c levels in diabetes care.
keyPoints: Chronic Care Model includes multiple strategies delivered by practice teams.
Effective strategies for lowering $HbA_{lc}$ include team changes and case management.
Cost-effectiveness may require a combination of strategies, influenced by pay-for-performance models.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.title: Public Reporting and Quality Metrics
items.content: Public reporting of quality data is increasingly common, with commercial websites providing access to regional and national hospital data. Despite limited patient use, these metrics influence provider behavior. However, challenges remain due to the potential for gaming metrics and reliance on claims data.
keyPoints: Quality data are publicly reported and affect provider behavior.
Patients rely more on provider reputation than quality metrics.
Metrics from EHRs are needed as many current metrics come from inaccurate claims data.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.title: Pay-for-Performance Incentives and Their Impact on Quality of Care
items.content: The pay-for-performance model aims to enhance quality by financially rewarding providers for higher-quality care. However, the effectiveness of this approach is still under investigation with limited evidence available. While incentives in the U.S. have been modest (1–2% of total payment), more aggressive models like those in the UK show notable improvements but raise concerns about potential negative outcomes such as patient selection bias.
keyPoints: Pay-for-performance aims to improve care quality by rewarding providers financially for higher-quality services.
Incentives in the U.S. have been modest, accounting for 1–2% of total payment.
The UK model places 40% of general practitioners' salaries at risk based on performance, showing substantial improvements.
Concerns include potential patient selection bias and overemphasis on incentivized quality measures.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3) | CONCLUSIONS

items.title: Challenges and Future Directions in Health Care Quality Improvement
items.content: Improving health care safety and quality is a priority, with many interventions showing promise. The shift towards value-based approaches like accountable care includes pay-for-performance linked to safety and quality. However, improvements also require structural changes such as team-oriented care and increased patient involvement. Safety measures are still developing, while existing quality measures cover only a small domain of overall quality.
keyPoints: Health care reform is likely to increase the use of value-based approaches including pay-for-performance.
Safety measures need further development for routine operational assessment at reasonable costs.
Quality improvement requires structural changes like team-oriented care and patient involvement.
Existing quality measures are more robust than safety measures but still cover a limited domain.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3) | CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: Which type of error occurs in new or nonstereotypic situations where conscious decisions are being made?
items.explanation: A mistake is an error of a higher level that occurs in new or nonstereotypic situations requiring conscious decisions.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: According to systems theory, what primarily causes most accidents in health care?
items.explanation: Systems theory suggests that most accidents result from defects in systems rather than individual negligence.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: What is a common effect of interruptions during health care delivery?
items.explanation: Interruptions can lead to forgetting to complete an action, increasing the likelihood of errors.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as increasing the likelihood of errors in health care?
items.explanation: Patient satisfaction is not listed as a factor that increases the likelihood of errors.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.question: According to the Harvard Medical Practice Study, what percentage of adverse events were considered preventable?
items.explanation: The study found that 58% of adverse events were considered preventable.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.question: What was the most common type of adverse event identified in the Harvard Medical Practice Study?
items.explanation: Adverse drug events (ADEs) were the most common, accounting for 19% of all adverse events.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of patients admitted to U.S. hospitals experience an ADE?
items.explanation: About 6–10% of patients admitted to U.S. hospitals experience an ADE.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.question: Which strategy has significantly reduced nosocomial infection rates in intensive care settings?
items.explanation: Implementation of checklists has greatly reduced nosocomial infection rates, especially for catheter-associated bloodstream infections and ventilator-associated pneumonia.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.question: What is one benefit of computerized physician order entry (CPOE) with clinical decision support?
options: It increases the rate of serious medication errors.
It decreases the serious medication error rate by 55%.
It has no effect on medication safety.
items.explanation: CPOE with clinical decision support decreased the serious medication error rate by 55% in one study.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.question: Which technology helps ensure that patients receive the right dose of medication based on their renal function?
items.explanation: Clinical decision support can suggest the right dose for a patient, tailoring it to the level of renal function.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.question: What is one practice recommended by the National Quality Forum to improve safety in hospitals?
items.explanation: 'Readback' is the practice of recording all verbal orders and immediately reading them back to verify accuracy.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.question: What challenge is associated with measuring the safety of care in hospitals?
options: Adverse events are rare, making measurement difficult and expensive.
Claims data work well for both surgical and medical care.
items.explanation: Measuring safety is challenging because adverse events are rare, and spontaneous reporting has low sensitivity (~1 in 20 ADEs reported).
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.question: According to Donabedian's quality theory, which of the following is NOT a type of measurement for assessing the quality of care?
items.explanation: Donabedian categorizes quality of care into structure, process, and outcome.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.question: What does the 'Process' category in Donabedian's model refer to?
options: The characteristics applicable in a setting
items.explanation: 'Process' refers to the way care is delivered, such as performing procedures at recommended intervals.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.question: Which statement best describes the relationship between structure and outcome in quality of health care?
options: Good structure always leads to good outcomes
Good structure does not always result in a good outcome
items.explanation: Even with good structure and process, the outcome may still be poor due to factors like severity of illness.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.question: What is a key tool mentioned for improving process performance in health care?
items.explanation: The Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle is a tool used for rapid improvement of processes in health care.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.question: Which strategy has been demonstrated to improve performance in delivering preventive services?
options: Introducing reminders for specific actions during patient visits
Using only claims data for quality measurement
items.explanation: Reminders about specific actions needed at a visit have been shown to improve preventive service delivery.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.question: What is the main focus of the Chronic Care Model developed by Wagner and colleagues?
options: Reducing hospital readmissions for surgical patients
items.explanation: The Chronic Care Model is focused on improving the quality of care for chronic diseases.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.question: Which strategies were found to be most effective in reducing hemoglobin A_{lc} levels in outpatient diabetes care?
options: Use of team changes and case managers
Exclusive use of decision support systems
items.explanation: The most effective strategies for reducing HbA_{lc} levels were the use of team changes and case managers.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.question: What is a significant change in quality measurement recently adopted by Medicare?
options: Switching to paper-based clinical quality metrics
Measuring care through claims data only
Using manual data extraction from EHRs
items.explanation: Medicare is moving to electronic clinical quality metrics (ECQMs) for measuring care.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following statements best describes a potential issue with pay-for-performance schemes in healthcare?
options: Providers may focus only on incentivized quality measures and neglect other aspects of care.
Pay-for-performance has been proven to be ineffective across all studies.
All providers universally agree that pay-for-performance is the best approach.
The majority of incentives currently account for over 50% of total payment in the United States.
items.explanation: Providers might focus too much on incentivized measures and ignore other quality aspects, leading to an incomplete quality picture.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3) | CONCLUSIONS

items.question: What is a concern when incentives are tied to outcomes in pay-for-performance schemes?
options: Providers may avoid taking on the sickest patients.
All providers will uniformly improve their performance.
There will be no need for further evaluation of these schemes.
The quality measures available will cover all aspects of care.
items.explanation: Tying incentives to outcomes may lead providers to transfer the sickest patients to other systems, avoiding high-risk cases.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3) | CONCLUSIONS

items.question: What is a likely trend under health care reform regarding pay-for-performance schemes?
options: They will be replaced entirely by traditional payment methods.
There will be no changes in their implementation.
The use of these schemes is expected to increase.
items.explanation: Under health care reform, the use of various pay-for-performance schemes is likely to increase.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3) | CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.statement: Slips are high-level errors occurring in new situations.
items.explanation: Slips are low-level, semiautomatic errors that occur during routine activities.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.statement: Transitions between providers can be points of vulnerability in health care.
items.explanation: Transitions between providers are common and represent points of vulnerability.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.statement: The frequency of harm related to medical care is now approaching one in four admissions.
items.explanation: Recent studies suggest that the frequency of harm related to medical care approaches one in four admissions.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.statement: The severity level of ADEs is higher in the outpatient setting compared to the inpatient setting.
items.explanation: The severity level of ADEs is lower in the outpatient setting than in the inpatient setting.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.statement: Bar coding linked with an electronic medication administration record can help ensure that the right patient receives the correct medication at the appropriate time.
items.explanation: Bar coding helps ensure patients receive the right medication at the right time.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.statement: The safety of health care cannot be improved substantially according to current studies.
items.explanation: It is clear that the safety of health care can be substantially improved.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.statement: Stress to providers can lead to improved quality by ensuring thoroughness.
items.explanation: Stress can decrease the level of quality as it may cause providers to omit important steps.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.statement: The RAND Corporation study found that patients in the United States received recommended care about half the time.
items.explanation: The study reported that only 55% of recommended care was delivered, equating to roughly a coin flip chance.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.statement: Public reporting of quality data has led patients to frequently use this information when choosing providers.
items.explanation: Patients have not made much use of public quality data; they rely more on provider reputation.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.statement: The Hospital Quality Initiative includes collaboration among several entities, such as the National Quality Forum.
items.explanation: The initiative is a collaboration involving multiple organizations including the National Quality Forum.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.statement: Pay-for-performance incentives in the United States have accounted for more than 50% of total payment.
items.explanation: Most quality-based performance incentives account for only 1–2% of total payment.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3) | CONCLUSIONS

items.statement: The science of quality improvement in healthcare is still developing.
items.explanation: The passage states that the science of quality improvement continues to mature.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3) | CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.assertion: Fatigue-related errors can be mitigated by limiting work hours.
items.reason: Extended-duty shifts are a significant cause of fatigue-related errors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.assertion: Complexity in health care systems does not contribute to errors.
items.reason: Providers deal with streams of data, some of which require action or suggest specific diagnoses.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; complexity contributes to errors. The reason is true.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · The Safety and Quality of Health Care | SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.assertion: Checklists have had a major impact on reducing catheter-associated bloodstream infections.
items.reason: The checklist concept ensures that specific actions are taken for every patient, which reduces the frequency of these complications.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why checklists have reduced infection rates.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.assertion: The use of triggers has decreased the frequency of adverse events.
items.reason: Triggers such as an unexpected transfer to the intensive care unit help identify adverse events more effectively.
items.explanation: While both statements are true, the reason does not explain a decrease in frequency; rather, it explains improved detection.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.assertion: Smart pumps are used to prevent medication errors by providing warnings for high doses.
items.reason: These pumps can be set according to the specific medication and dose, alerting health care professionals if a too-high dose is about to be administered.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.assertion: Most hospitals have a complete understanding of the frequency of safety issues.
items.reason: Hospitals rely on spontaneous reporting to identify errors and adverse events, which is highly sensitive.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because hospitals have little idea about the true frequency of safety issues; the reason is also false as spontaneous reporting has low sensitivity.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · SAFETY IN HEALTH CARE (part 3)

items.assertion: Improving the performance level of all providers is more effective for overall quality improvement than punishing poor performers.
items.reason: Systems changes can affect large numbers of providers simultaneously, leading to broader improvements.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why improving performance levels broadly is beneficial.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.assertion: High production pressure always leads to better quality of care.
items.reason: Providers under high production pressure may omit important steps, reducing the quality of care.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; high production pressure can decrease quality by causing providers to skip crucial steps.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.assertion: Quality metrics can be 'gamed' and often come from claims with inaccuracies.
items.reason: Most quality metrics are derived from claims data, which contain many inaccuracies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why metrics can be gamed.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.assertion: SCAMPs were developed by patients to improve care outcomes.
items.reason: SCAMPs are standardized clinical assessment and management plans created by clinicians.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; SCAMPs were developed by clinicians, not patients.
items.reference: The Safety and Quality of Health Care · QUALITY IN HEALTH CARE (part 2)
